# Kubernetes APF QueueSet: Algorithmic Deep-Dive

## Table of Contents

1. [Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries](#1-subsystem-purpose-and-boundaries)
2. [Component Responsibilities](#2-component-responsibilities)
3. [End-to-End Request Flow](#3-end-to-end-request-flow)
4. [The Fair Queuing Algorithm in Detail](#4-the-fair-queuing-algorithm-in-detail)
5. [Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs](#5-failure-modes-limits-and-design-tradeoffs)
6. [Extension Points and Customization Hooks](#6-extension-points-and-customization-hooks)
7. [Operational Implications](#7-operational-implications)
8. [File Map](#8-file-map)

---

## 1. Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries

### Purpose

The APF (API Priority and Fairness) QueueSet subsystem implements **Fair Queuing for Server Requests** inside the Kubernetes API server. Its job is to prevent any single client or flow from monopolizing the apiserver's concurrency budget, while still making forward progress for all active flows under overload.

Every non-exempt apiserver request is routed through a *priority level*, each of which owns exactly one `QueueSet`. The `QueueSet` for a priority level is the gate that decides:

- whether an incoming request can be dispatched immediately (seats available),
- whether it must wait in a queue (backpressure),
- whether it must be rejected outright (queue full and concurrency limit exceeded), or
- whether it times out while waiting (context cancelled).

### Scope Boundary

The QueueSet subsystem is responsible only for **queuing and dispatch decisions** within a single priority level. It has no knowledge of:

- how requests are classified (FlowSchema matching happens in `apf_controller.go`);
- cross-level concurrency borrowing (also handled at the controller layer);
- HTTP-level admission or request routing.

### Upstream Dependencies

| Dependency | Role |
|---|---|
| `configController` (`apf_controller.go`) | Creates and reconfigures QueueSets; provides `ConcurrencyLimit` and queuing parameters |
| `FlowSchema` / `PriorityLevelConfiguration` API objects | Supply `HandSize`, `Queues`, `QueueLengthLimit`, `NominalConcurrencyShares`, and borrow limits |
| `WorkEstimator` (`request/width.go`) | Computes `InitialSeats`, `FinalSeats`, `AdditionalLatency` for each request before `StartRequest` is called |
| `shufflesharding.Dealer` (`pkg/util/shufflesharding/shufflesharding.go`) | Generates a hand of candidate queue indices from a hash value |
| `eventclock.Interface` (`fairqueuing/eventclock/interface.go`) | Real-time clock and deferred-event scheduling (used for `AdditionalLatency` seat release) |

### Downstream Consumers

| Consumer | What it receives |
|---|---|
| `configController.Handle` (`apf_filter.go:155`) | A `fq.Request` handle; calls `Finish(execFn)` to block until dispatched or rejected |
| Prometheus metrics subsystem | Per-level/schema counters for waiting, executing, dispatched, rejected, seat occupancy, virtual time |
| Debug endpoint (`/debug/api_priority_and_fairness/...`) | Live `QueueSetDump` snapshots produced by `Dump()` |

---

## 2. Component Responsibilities

### 2.1 `QueueSetFactory` / `queueSetFactory`

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/queueset.go:40-45`

A factory that holds a shared `eventclock.Interface` and a `promiseFactoryFactory`. Its sole job is `BeginConstruction`, which validates the `QueuingConfig`, creates a `shufflesharding.Dealer`, and returns a `QueueSetCompleter`. Construction is split into two phases so that validation errors from queuing parameters (phase 1) are surfaced before committing a concurrency allotment (phase 2):

```
queueSetFactory.BeginConstruction(QueuingConfig, ...) -> QueueSetCompleter
QueueSetCompleter.Complete(DispatchingConfig) -> QueueSet
```

This two-phase pattern is reused for reconfiguration: `QueueSet.BeginConfigChange(QueuingConfig)` returns a new `QueueSetCompleter` linked to the existing `queueSet`, and `Complete` writes new configuration atomically under the lock.

### 2.2 `queueSet` — Central State Machine

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/queueset.go:85-167`

The `queueSet` struct is the heart of the implementation. Key fields (lock-protected unless noted):

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `queues []*queue` | Slice of per-flow queues; may exceed `DesiredNumQueues` while old queues drain |
| `currentR SeatSeconds` | Global virtual-time progress meter R (seat-seconds allocated per queue since process start) |
| `lastRealTime time.Time` | Wall clock at the last virtual-time sync |
| `robinIndex int` | Round-robin cursor for the dispatch scan |
| `totSeatsInUse int` | Total seats currently occupied by executing requests |
| `totSeatsWaiting int` | Sum of `MaxSeats` over all waiting requests |
| `dealer *shufflesharding.Dealer` | Immutable for the life of a config; replaced on reconfigure |
| `estimatedServiceDuration` | Fixed at 3ms (line 216); used in virtual-time rate and anti-windup calculations |
| `promiseFactory` | Produces `WriteOnce` promises for per-request dispatch decisions |

All methods with suffix `Locked` require the mutex; those without either acquire it or are lock-agnostic.

### 2.3 `queue` — Per-Flow Queue

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/types.go:92-110`

Each `queue` tracks:

- `requestsWaiting fifo` — a doubly-linked FIFO list (`requestFIFO`, backed by `container/list`) holding waiting `*request` values, plus an aggregated `queueSum` (seat sums and total-work sum maintained incrementally on enqueue/dequeue).
- `requestsExecuting sets.Set[*request]` — the set of requests from this queue currently in the execution phase.
- `nextDispatchR SeatSeconds` — the virtual-time cursor for this queue; the R value at which the next request from this queue will be dispatched in the virtual world. This is the key per-queue state for the SRPT-style fair queuing.
- `seatsInUse int` — seats occupied by currently executing requests from this queue (used in virtual-time rate computation).

### 2.4 `request` — Per-Request Envelope

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/types.go:33-83`

Wraps one in-flight request. Notable fields:

- `decision promise.WriteOnce` — a write-once channel-like object. Set to `decisionExecute` by the dispatcher, or set to `decisionCancel` by context cancellation. The goroutine that called `Finish` blocks in `decision.Get()` until one of these happens.
- `workEstimate completedWorkEstimate` — holds `InitialSeats`, `FinalSeats`, `AdditionalLatency`, `totalWork` (seat-seconds), and `finalWork`.
- `arrivalR SeatSeconds` — value of `currentR` when this request arrived; used by the anti-windup hack.
- `removeFromQueueLocked removeFromFIFOFunc` — O(1) closure to remove the request from its queue's FIFO (uses the `list.Element` pointer directly).

### 2.5 `promise.WriteOnce`

**File:** `fairqueuing/promise/promise.go`, `fairqueuing/promise/interface.go`

A minimal write-once synchronization primitive backed by a channel. `Set` uses `sync.Once` to guarantee idempotence; `Get` blocks on either the set channel or the context's `Done` channel, whichever fires first. When the context fires, `Get` calls `Set(doneVal)` (which is `decisionCancel`) before returning, ensuring `Set` is idempotent even in the cancellation race.

The key property that makes the locking strategy work: `Set` is always called while holding `qs.lock`, while `Get` is called without the lock (blocking the caller's goroutine independently). This means the dispatcher can write the decision and update queue state atomically, while the waiting goroutine unblocks only after the lock is released.

### 2.6 `requestFIFO`

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/fifo_list.go`

A `container/list`-backed FIFO with O(1) removal. `Enqueue` returns a closure holding the `*list.Element`; calling it removes the element and updates the `queueSum` aggregate in O(1). `Walk` supports safe concurrent-removal iteration (pre-fetches `current.Next()` before calling `f`).

### 2.7 `shufflesharding.Dealer`

**File:** `pkg/util/shufflesharding/shufflesharding.go`

Implements the shuffle-sharding card-deal algorithm. Given a 64-bit hash and parameters `(deckSize=numQueues, handSize=HandSize)`, it produces a hand of `HandSize` distinct queue indices using Knuth's partial shuffle. The entropy required is `ceil(log2(deckSize) * handSize)` bits, capped at 60 bits of the hash to avoid bias in the last card.

### 2.8 `configController` (APF layer above QueueSet)

**Files:** `apf_controller.go`, `apf_filter.go`

The `configController` sits above the QueueSet subsystem. It:

- watches `FlowSchema` and `PriorityLevelConfiguration` API objects via informers, and rebuilds `priorityLevelStates` on any change (`digestConfigObjects`);
- owns one `priorityLevelState` per priority level, each containing a `fq.QueueSet`;
- runs a periodic borrowing adjustment loop every 10 seconds (`updateBorrowing`), which reads smoothed seat-demand statistics from each level's `seatDemandIntegrator`, solves an allocation problem (`computeConcurrencyAllocation`), and calls `Complete(DispatchingConfig{ConcurrencyLimit: currentCL})` to push the new limit into the live QueueSet — without recreating it.

### 2.9 `Integrator`

**File:** `fairqueuing/integrator.go`

A time-weighted statistical integrator (tracks mean, standard deviation, min, max of a time-series). Each priority level attaches one to its QueueSet's `seatDemandIntegrator` gauge. On each state change (request enters/leaves queue or execution), the QueueSet calls `seatDemandIntegrator.Set(totSeatsInUse + totSeatsWaiting)`. The controller's borrowing loop calls `Reset()` every 10 seconds to harvest statistics and reset the window.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

The flow from an incoming HTTP request to execution and completion involves five distinct stages.

### Stage 0: Classification (above QueueSet)

In `configController.startRequest` (`apf_controller.go:1022`):

1. The `flowSchemas` slice (sorted by matching precedence) is scanned linearly until a `FlowSchema` matches the request's `RequestInfo` and `User`.
2. The matched FlowSchema identifies a priority level name; the corresponding `priorityLevelState` is looked up.
3. If the priority level uses queuing (`LimitResponseTypeQueue`) and has more than one queue, a `flowDistinguisher` string is computed (by user name or namespace, per `DistinguisherMethod`) and hashed with the FlowSchema name via SHA-256:
   ```go
   // apf_controller.go:1131
   hash := sha256.New()
   hash.Write([]byte(fsName)); hash.Write(sep[:]); hash.Write([]byte(fDistinguisher))
   hashValue = binary.LittleEndian.Uint64(sum[:8])
   ```
4. The `WorkEstimator` is called to produce `InitialSeats`, `FinalSeats`, `AdditionalLatency`.
5. `plState.queues.StartRequest(ctx, &workEstimate, hashValue, ...)` is called — entering the QueueSet.

### Stage 1: `StartRequest` — Queue Assignment and Enqueue

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/queueset.go:308`

Under the QueueSet's mutex (acquired via `lockAndSyncTime` which also advances virtual time):

**Path A — no queuing** (`DesiredNumQueues < 1`):

`canAccommodateSeatsLocked` checks `totSeatsInUse + request.MaxSeats() <= ConcurrencyLimit`. If it fails, the request is rejected immediately with reason `"concurrency-limit"` and `nil` is returned. If it passes, `dispatchSansQueueLocked` is called, which creates the request with `decision` pre-set to `decisionExecute`, increments executing counters, and returns immediately.

**Path B — with queuing** (`DesiredNumQueues >= 1`):

1. **Shuffle-sharding** (`shuffleShardLocked`, line 583): The dealer deals `HandSize` cards (queue indices) from the hash. The QueueSet iterates the hand starting at a rotating offset (`qs.enqueues % handSize`) to remove ordering bias, picks the queue with the minimum `TotalWorkSum` (total seat-seconds of waiting work), and selects it as the target.

2. **Reject or enqueue** (`rejectOrEnqueueToBoundLocked`, line 618): Rejection fires only if *both* conditions hold simultaneously:
   ```
   totSeatsInUse >= ConcurrencyLimit  AND  queue.requestsWaiting.Length() >= QueueLengthLimit
   ```
   If either condition is not met, the request is enqueued in the selected queue's FIFO. The `arrivalR` is set to `currentR` at enqueue time; if the queue was idle, `nextDispatchR` is also reset to `currentR` (avoiding an empty queue inheriting a stale virtual start).

3. **Dispatch loop** (`dispatchAsMuchAsPossibleLocked`, line 654): After enqueue, this loop fires and may immediately dispatch the newly enqueued request or other waiting requests:
   ```go
   for qs.totRequestsWaiting != 0 && qs.totSeatsInUse < qs.dCfg.ConcurrencyLimit && qs.dispatchLocked() {
   }
   ```

### Stage 2: Dispatch Selection (`dispatchLocked` / `findDispatchQueueToBoundLocked`)

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/queueset.go:695-843`

The dispatcher selects the best queue to dispatch from using a **virtual-finish-time** criterion, equivalent to Shortest Remaining Processing Time across queues:

```
virtualFinish(queue) = queue.nextDispatchR + oldestRequest.totalWork()
```

The scan iterates all queues in round-robin order (resuming from `robinIndex`) and finds the queue with the minimum `virtualFinish`. This biases toward queues that have been waiting longest in virtual time, achieving fairness across flows.

After selecting the queue:

- `canAccommodateSeatsLocked` is called for the head request. If `request.MaxSeats() > ConcurrencyLimit` and there are currently executing requests, dispatch stalls — the loop exits and will retry when a request finishes.
- The head request is dequeued (O(1) via its `removeFromQueueLocked` closure).
- `FinalSeats` are clamped to `ConcurrencyLimit` if they would exceed it, with `AdditionalLatency` adjusted to preserve total work.
- **Virtual time is advanced**: `queue.nextDispatchR += request.totalWork()` (seat-seconds consumed in the virtual world). This is the core progress accounting: a queue that dispatches more work accumulates a higher `nextDispatchR`, naturally reducing its priority for subsequent dispatches.
- `request.decision.Set(decisionExecute)` is called under the lock, unblocking the waiting goroutine.

### Stage 3: Waiting (`request.wait`)

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/queueset.go:397`

The goroutine that called `StartRequest` (and received a non-nil `Request`) calls `req.Finish(execFn)`, which internally calls `req.wait()`:

```go
decisionAny := req.decision.Get()  // blocks here until dispatched or cancelled
```

`Get` blocks on `select { case <-p.setCh: ... case <-p.doneCtx.Done(): ... }`. If the context fires first, `Set(decisionCancel)` races with the dispatcher's `Set(decisionExecute)` — only one wins (via `sync.Once`). After returning from `Get`, `wait` acquires the lock to finalize state:

- `decisionExecute`: return `(true, false)` — proceed to execution.
- `decisionCancel`: if the request is still in its queue (i.e., `removeFromQueueLocked()` returns non-nil), it is removed, counters are decremented, and reason `"time-out"` is recorded in metrics.

### Stage 4: Execution and Seat Release (`finishRequestLocked`)

**File:** `fairqueuing/queueset/queueset.go:863`

After `execFn()` returns, `finishRequestLocked` is called:

1. The request is removed from `queue.requestsExecuting`.
2. **Virtual-time correction**: The queue's `nextDispatchR` is adjusted for the difference between estimated and actual service time:
   ```go
   queue.nextDispatchR -= SeatsTimesDuration(float64(r.InitialSeats()), estimatedServiceDuration - actualServiceDuration)
   ```
   This retroactively corrects the virtual-time ledger: if actual service was shorter than the 3ms estimate, `nextDispatchR` is wound back, giving this queue's next request an earlier virtual start than it would otherwise have had.
3. `boundNextDispatchLocked` (the anti-windup hack) is then applied: if `nextDispatchR` fell below the oldest waiting request's `arrivalR`, it is clamped up to `arrivalR`. This prevents a queue from accumulating an unfairly early virtual start when it is lightly loaded.
4. **Seat release — immediate or deferred**: If `AdditionalLatency <= 0`, seats are released immediately. Otherwise, `qs.clock.EventAfterDuration(releaseSeatsLocked, additionalLatency)` schedules seat release in a new goroutine after the additional latency. This models requests (e.g., watches) that hold server resources beyond their initial response.
5. `removeQueueIfEmptyLocked` removes queues that are both empty and in excess of `DesiredNumQueues` (queue shrinkage by attrition).
6. `dispatchAsMuchAsPossibleLocked` is called to dispatch any newly eligible requests.

---

## 4. The Fair Queuing Algorithm in Detail

### Virtual Time (`currentR`)

`currentR` is a global seat-seconds counter, advanced in `syncTimeLocked` every time the lock is acquired:

```go
// queueset.go:470
incrR = SeatsTimesDuration(getVirtualTimeRatioLocked(), timeSinceLast)
qs.currentR = prevR + incrR
```

The **virtual time ratio** (`getVirtualTimeRatioLocked`, line 526) is:

```
ratio = min(sum(seatsRequested across active queues), ConcurrencyLimit) / activeQueues
```

where `seatsRequested` for a queue is `seatsInUse + MaxSeatsSum(waiting)`. This models each active queue as receiving an equal share of the concurrency budget — the foundation of the fairness guarantee.

When no queues are active, the ratio is 0 and virtual time freezes (no work is being done).

### Queue Selection for Dispatch

The dispatcher selects the queue minimizing:

```
nextDispatchR + totalWork(head request)
```

`nextDispatchR` reflects how much virtual-time work this queue has already been allocated. Adding the head request's work gives the virtual finish time of the next item if dispatched from this queue. Choosing the minimum ensures the queue that has received the least service (in virtual time) gets priority — weighted by request size.

### Work Units: `SeatSeconds`

**File:** `request/seat_seconds.go`

`SeatSeconds` is a fixed-point `uint64` with scale factor `1e8` (i.e., 1 seat-second = `1e8` units). This avoids floating-point arithmetic in the hot path while providing nanosecond-scale granularity for the 3ms estimated service duration.

`totalWork` for a request is:

```
totalWork = SeatsTimesDuration(InitialSeats, estimatedServiceDuration) + SeatsTimesDuration(FinalSeats, AdditionalLatency)
```

This means a request that uses more seats or has additional latency consumes proportionally more virtual-time budget, correctly penalizing heavy requests.

### Epoch Advancement (Overflow Prevention)

**File:** `queueset.go:489-522`

`currentR` and all `nextDispatchR` / `arrivalR` values are `uint64`. To prevent overflow, when `currentR >= highR` (approximately 3/4 of `MaxSeatSeconds`), `advanceEpoch` subtracts `rDecrement = MaxSeatSeconds/2` from `currentR` and all per-queue and per-request readings. Idle queues have their `nextDispatchR` reset to 0 (safe because their value is stale). Overflow in the subtraction (underflow of unsigned values) is detected and logged.

---

## 5. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 5.1 Queue Full Rejection

A request is rejected with `"queue-full"` if and only if the selected queue is full **and** the QueueSet is at its concurrency limit simultaneously (`rejectOrEnqueueToBoundLocked`, line 618). The "and" is deliberate: if seats are available, requests are still accepted even if queues are deep, since they can be dispatched immediately. This avoids spurious rejection under bursty load.

**Operational risk**: Misconfigured `QueueLengthLimit` (too low) causes premature rejection under legitimate load spikes. Too high wastes memory and allows unbounded latency accumulation.

### 5.2 Oversized Requests

If `request.MaxSeats() > ConcurrencyLimit` and other requests are executing, the request cannot be dispatched — `canAccommodateSeatsLocked` returns false and the dispatch loop stalls. The request waits in its queue. If the request is the *only* thing in the system (`totRequestsExecuting == 0`), it is allowed through anyway to avoid starvation.

**Tradeoff**: This prevents seat arithmetic overflow, but a single oversized request can temporarily block an entire QueueSet.

### 5.3 Virtual Time Windup (Anti-Windup Hack)

A queue that receives no requests for a long time has a stale `nextDispatchR` (from its last dispatch). When it later receives a request, `nextDispatchR` could be far in the past relative to `currentR`, making this queue appear to be owed an enormous amount of service. The anti-windup `boundNextDispatchLocked` (line 952) clamps `nextDispatchR` upward to `oldestWaiting.arrivalR`:

```go
if queue.nextDispatchR < virtualStartBound {
    queue.nextDispatchR = virtualStartBound
}
```

**Tradeoff**: This is a pragmatic approximation. It prevents windfall service for bursty flows but means a flow that goes idle and returns cannot "bank" its unused capacity — each flow gets at most its fair share going forward.

### 5.4 `estimatedServiceDuration` Is a Fixed Constant

`estimatedServiceDuration` is hardcoded at 3ms (`queueset.go:216`). This estimate is used in:

- Computing `totalWork` for virtual-time bookkeeping (the initial-phase component).
- Computing `estimatedWorkInProgress` in `findDispatchQueueToBoundLocked` (for the `dsMin`/`dsMax` metrics).
- Post-execution `nextDispatchR` correction: if actual service took 10ms instead of 3ms, `nextDispatchR` is reduced by `7ms * InitialSeats`.

**Risk**: If actual request service times are systematically far from 3ms (e.g., long-running list requests), the virtual-time accounting during queuing is inaccurate. The post-execution correction partially compensates but applies only retroactively.

### 5.5 Borrowing and the `ConcurrencyLimit` Race

The `ConcurrencyLimit` for a QueueSet is updated every 10 seconds by the borrowing loop via `Complete(DispatchingConfig{ConcurrencyLimit: currentCL})`. `Complete` calls `setConfiguration`, which calls `dispatchAsMuchAsPossibleLocked` — meaning the new limit takes effect immediately for dispatch decisions. Between adjustment periods, a priority level with a temporarily lower limit may queue requests that would have been admitted under the old limit.

**Tradeoff**: The 10-second borrowing period provides smoothing (via `seatDemandSmoothingCoefficient = 0.977`, half-life ~5 minutes) against transient load spikes, at the cost of slow adaptation. A sudden traffic surge takes minutes to fully propagate to increased concurrency for a needy level.

### 5.6 `AdditionalLatency` and Deferred Seat Release

Watch requests and large list responses that hold server-side resources after the response headers are sent are modeled with `FinalSeats` and `AdditionalLatency`. The `EventAfterDuration` mechanism schedules seat release asynchronously, so the response is delivered to the client while APF continues to account for the held seats. This correctly models resource consumption but adds complexity:

- A goroutine is spawned per request with non-zero `AdditionalLatency`.
- Seat release may race with QueueSet reconfiguration.
- Metrics (`execSeatsGauge`) remain elevated during the additional latency, which is correct but may surprise operators reading dashboards.

### 5.7 Lock Contention

The entire `queueSet` is protected by a single `sync.Mutex`. Every call to `StartRequest`, `Finish`, and the `EventAfterDuration` callback acquires this lock. Under high request rates this becomes a serialization point. The `syncTimeLocked` call at every lock acquisition advances virtual time — adding a small but constant overhead proportional to time elapsed.

---

## 6. Extension Points and Customization Hooks

### 6.1 `QueueSetFactory` Interface

**File:** `fairqueuing/interface.go:32`

Production code uses `fqs.NewQueueSetFactory(clk)` (`apf_filter.go:105`). Tests may substitute a `newTestableQueueSetFactory` with a different `promiseFactoryFactory`. The interface is fully replaceable, but in practice the only real implementation is `queueSetFactory` in the `queueset` package.

**Risk**: Replacing the factory is not a supported operator configuration; it requires Go-level code changes.

### 6.2 `promiseFactoryFactory` — Testability Hook

**File:** `queueset.go:47-54`

The `promiseFactoryFactory` is a function that, given a `*queueSet`, returns a factory for `WriteOnce` promises. The production factory returns `promise.NewWriteOnce`. Test code can substitute a fake that allows deterministic control of when dispatch decisions are delivered.

This hook exists purely to make the dispatch-wait race testable without real goroutines and wall-clock time.

### 6.3 `eventclock.Interface` — Clock Abstraction

**File:** `fairqueuing/eventclock/interface.go`

All time-based operations go through `eventclock.Interface` (which embeds `clock.PassiveClock`). Tests supply a `FakeClock` that controls time and deferred events manually. The real implementation (`eventclock.Real`) wraps standard Go time functions.

**Extension risk**: The clock is wired at `New()` time (`apf_filter.go:94`, `Real{}`). Operators cannot substitute a custom clock without modifying the source.

### 6.4 `WorkEstimator` — Pluggable Request Sizing

**File:** `request/width.go:75`

`NewWorkEstimator` takes four parameters:

- `objectCountFn` — returns the stored object count for a resource (used by the list estimator).
- `watchCountFn` — returns the number of interested watchers for a request (used by the mutating estimator, which accounts for watch fan-out).
- `config *WorkEstimatorConfig` — minimum/maximum seat limits.
- `maxSeatsFn` — returns the per-priority-level maximum seats (tracked by `MaxSeatsTracker`, set at 15% of `nominalCL`, `apf_controller.go:63`).

Operators can influence work estimation indirectly through `PriorityLevelConfiguration.spec.limited.limitResponse.queuing` fields and by the object count and watch count functions, which are populated from storage backends.

**Risk**: The work estimator is called while holding `cfgCtlr.lock.RLock()` (inside `startRequest`). A slow `objectCountFn` or `watchCountFn` would hold the read lock and stall all concurrent classification.

### 6.5 `FlowDistinguisherMethod` — Flow Partitioning

**File:** `apf_controller.go:1115-1129`

The `flowDistinguisher` fed into hash computation is determined by `DistinguisherMethod.Type`:

- `ByUser` — uses `rd.User.GetName()`
- `ByNamespace` — uses `rd.RequestInfo.Namespace`

This is the only operator-controlled knob that affects which queue within a priority level a given request lands in. It is set per FlowSchema in the API object. A single-queue priority level ignores it entirely (no shuffle-sharding).

**Tradeoff**: `ByUser` provides isolation between users of the same priority level; `ByNamespace` provides isolation between namespaces. Neither provides isolation across multiple dimensions simultaneously.

### 6.6 `QueueSet.Dump` — Debug Introspection

**File:** `queueset.go:996`

`Dump(includeRequestDetails bool)` acquires the lock, snapshots all queue state, and returns a `debug.QueueSetDump`. With `includeRequestDetails=true` it also extracts `UserInfo` and `RequestInfo` from the request contexts. This is exposed via the APF debug HTTP endpoint installed by `Install(mux)`.

**Risk**: `Dump` holds the QueueSet lock for the duration of the snapshot. On a large, busy QueueSet this can cause momentary stalls to all concurrent requests.

---

## 7. Operational Implications

### 7.1 Diagnosing Rejections

Three rejection reasons appear in `apiserver_flowcontrol_rejected_requests_total`:

| Reason | Source | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `concurrency-limit` | `StartRequest` path A | No-queue priority level hit its seat limit |
| `queue-full` | `StartRequest` path B | Both queue-length and seat-limit exceeded simultaneously |
| `time-out` | `request.wait` | Request context cancelled while waiting |

`queue-full` rejections are the primary signal of overload at a priority level. `time-out` rejections indicate clients with short deadlines experiencing sustained queue depth. Neither is necessarily pathological — some rejection is expected and healthy under overload — but sustained high rates warrant attention.

### 7.2 Monitoring Virtual Time

`apiserver_flowcontrol_current_r` (set via `metrics.SetCurrentR`) tracks `currentR` per priority level. A monotonically advancing `currentR` indicates the priority level is active. A `currentR` that advances very slowly relative to other levels indicates few active queues or low seat demand.

`apiserver_flowcontrol_dispatch_r`, `apiserver_flowcontrol_latest_s`, and related metrics (set via `metrics.SetDispatchMetrics`) expose `minVirtualFinish`, `sMin`, `sMax`, `dsMin`, `dsMax` from `findDispatchQueueToBoundLocked`. These are useful for understanding the spread of virtual times across queues — a large `sMax - sMin` indicates significant imbalance in how much service different flows have received.

### 7.3 Seat Demand and Borrowing Observability

`apiserver_flowcontrol_priority_level_seat_count_samples` tracks the time-weighted integral of seat demand per priority level. The borrowing loop reads this every 10 seconds and adjusts `currentCL`. `apiserver_flowcontrol_lower_limit_seats`, `apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats`, and `apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats` expose the bounds within which `currentCL` can move.

If a priority level is consistently borrowing (its `currentCL` is above its `nominalCL`), other levels are lending it capacity — verify that the lenders are not starved.

### 7.4 The 3ms Estimation Problem in Practice

Because `estimatedServiceDuration = 3ms` is fixed, list requests that complete in 500ms inflate a queue's `nextDispatchR` by only `InitialSeats * 3ms` during queuing. The post-execution correction applies after the fact. This means:

- During a queue backup, the virtual-time selection algorithm underestimates the true cost of list requests.
- Queues containing large lists may be over-dispatched before the correction kicks in.
- The `WorkEstimate.FinalSeats + AdditionalLatency` portion is accounted correctly in virtual time, since `AdditionalLatency` is a real measured quantity computed by the work estimator from watch fan-out.

### 7.5 Configuration Stability Concerns

Every change to any `FlowSchema` or `PriorityLevelConfiguration` object triggers a full re-digest of all config objects (`lockAndDigestConfigObjects`). During this re-digest, `cfgCtlr.lock` is held in write mode, blocking all concurrent `startRequest` calls (which need the read lock). This is bounded by informer event rate but can cause brief stalls under heavy automated config churn (e.g., frequent operator or controller writes).

When `DesiredNumQueues` decreases, excess queues are not discarded immediately — they drain by attrition (`removeQueueIfEmptyLocked`). During draining, the queue count exceeds `DesiredNumQueues`, which means shuffle-sharding deals from more queues than configured, slightly reducing isolation guarantees until all excess queues are empty.

### 7.6 Memory Budget

Each `queue` holds a `requestFIFO` of waiting `*request` values plus a `sets.Set[*request]` of executing ones. With `N` priority levels each having `Q` queues and `L` max queue length, the worst-case waiting-request memory is `N * Q * L * sizeof(request)`. A `request` struct carries its context, work estimate, and two `interface{}` descriptors — roughly 200–400 bytes depending on context size. For typical APF configs (e.g., 64 queues, 50-item limit), this is negligible, but operators who configure very large queue depths should account for memory usage under pathological overload.

---

## 8. File Map

```
staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/
│
├── apf_filter.go                    Interface definition, Handle() entry point,
│                                    New() / NewTestable() constructors
│
├── apf_controller.go                configController: FlowSchema classification,
│                                    priority level state management, borrowing loop,
│                                    startRequest(), hashFlowID(),
│                                    queueSetCompleterForPL(), digestConfigObjects()
│
├── conc_alloc.go                    computeConcurrencyAllocation(): linear-programming
│                                    solver for the per-level concurrency distribution
│                                    problem used by the borrowing loop
│
├── dropped_requests_tracker.go      RetryAfter header advisor: tracks rejected
│                                    requests per second and suggests backoff
│
├── request/
│   ├── width.go                     WorkEstimate struct, WorkEstimatorFunc,
│   │                                NewWorkEstimator(), MaxSeats()
│   ├── seat_seconds.go              SeatSeconds fixed-point type and arithmetic
│   ├── list_work_estimator.go       Seat estimation for list requests (object count)
│   └── mutating_work_estimator.go   Seat estimation for mutating requests (watch fan-out)
│
└── fairqueuing/
    │
    ├── interface.go                 QueueSetFactory, QueueSetCompleter, QueueSet,
    │                                Request, QueueNoteFn, QueuingConfig,
    │                                DispatchingConfig interfaces and types
    │
    ├── integrator.go                Integrator: time-weighted mean/stddev/min/max
    │                                of a signal; used for seat-demand statistics
    │                                fed into the borrowing loop
    │
    ├── queueset/
    │   ├── queueset.go              queueSetFactory, queueSet, queueSetCompleter:
    │   │                            StartRequest, dispatchAsMuchAsPossibleLocked,
    │   │                            dispatchLocked, findDispatchQueueToBoundLocked,
    │   │                            shuffleShardLocked, finishRequestLocked,
    │   │                            syncTimeLocked, advanceEpoch,
    │   │                            boundNextDispatchLocked, Dump
    │   │

    │   │                            work arithmetic helpers
    │   │
    │   └── fifo_list.go             fifo interface, requestFIFO (container/list
    │                                backed), O(1) removeFromFIFOFunc closure,

    │

pkg/util/shufflesharding/
```

# Task: Kubernetes APF QueueSet Deep-Dive

**Repository:** kubernetes/kubernetes (stripped snapshot)
**Output:** Write your document to `/workspace/documentation.md`

## Objective

Produce an algorithmic deep-dive on APF QueueSet behavior, dispatch flow, and fairness tradeoffs.

## Scope

Focus on the APF QueueSet subsystem.
Your document must explain component responsibilities, end-to-end flow, and extension or operational tradeoffs.

## Content Expectations

Address all of the following in your own structure:
- subsystem purpose, boundaries, and upstream/downstream dependencies
- key components and how responsibilities are split
- end-to-end control/request flow with concrete repository evidence
- failure modes, limits, and design tradeoffs
- extension points, customization hooks, and associated risks
- a concise map of the most important files/directories used in your analysis

## Quality Bar

- Cite concrete file paths from the repository.
- Explain behavior and interactions, not just API signatures.
- Include at least one section on operational implications (performance, reliability, or maintainability).
- Do not fabricate classes, functions, or files.

## Anti-Requirements

- Do not output `doc.go`.
- Do not provide shallow bullet dumps without system flow explanation.
- Do not rely on external docs not present in the workspace.
Now I have a thorough understanding of the subsystem. Let me also check the debug package and the dropped requests tracker for completeness.
Now I have enough information to write the comprehensive documentation. Let me write it:

The document has been written to `/workspace/documentation.md`. Here is a summary of what it covers:

---

**Document structure:**

1. **Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries** — Establishes that each non-exempt priority level owns exactly one QueueSet; defines what is and is not the QueueSet's job; maps upstream dependencies (configController, WorkEstimator, shufflesharding, eventclock) and downstream consumers (Handle, Prometheus, debug endpoint).

2. **Component Responsibilities** — Details each structural piece:
   - `queueSetFactory` / two-phase construction pattern
   - `queueSet` central state machine and its lock-protected fields
   - `queue` per-flow FIFO with aggregated `queueSum`
   - `request` per-request envelope and the `decision` promise
   - `promise.WriteOnce` and its locking contract (Set under lock, Get without lock)
   - `requestFIFO` O(1) closure-based removal
   - `shufflesharding.Dealer` and entropy requirements
   - `configController` and the 10-second borrowing loop
   - `Integrator` for time-weighted seat-demand statistics

3. **End-to-End Request Flow** — Five concrete stages with file+line citations: classification and hash computation, queue assignment via shuffle-sharding, virtual-finish-time dispatch selection, the promise-based goroutine blocking pattern, and two-phase seat release with `AdditionalLatency`.

4. **Fair Queuing Algorithm** — Explains `currentR` / virtual time rate, why `min(seatsRequested, ConcurrencyLimit) / activeQueues` achieves fairness, how `totalWork = InitialSeats×3ms + FinalSeats×AdditionalLatency` weights heavy requests, and the epoch-advancement overflow mechanism.

5. **Failure Modes and Tradeoffs** — Queue-full rejection semantics, oversized-request stall, anti-windup limitations, the hardcoded 3ms estimate problem, borrowing lag, `AdditionalLatency` goroutine overhead, and single-lock contention.

6. **Extension Points** — The factory interface, `promiseFactoryFactory` testability hook, clock abstraction, pluggable `WorkEstimator`, `FlowDistinguisherMethod` knobs, and the `Dump` introspection mechanism.

7. **Operational Implications** — How to interpret rejection metrics, virtual-time gauges, seat-demand/borrowing metrics, the practical impact of the 3ms estimate, config-change stalls during re-digest, and memory budget under large queue configurations.

8. **File Map** — A concise annotated tree of every source file used in the analysis.

    14→limitations under the License.

    16→
    17→package queueset
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"context"
    21→	"time"
    22→
    23→	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/util/sets"
    24→	genericrequest "k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/request"
    25→	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/debug"
    26→	fq "k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/fairqueuing"
    27→	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/fairqueuing/promise"
    28→	fcrequest "k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/request"
    29→)
    30→
    31→// request is a temporary container for "requests" with additional
    32→// tracking fields required for QueueSet functionality.
    33→type request struct {
    34→	ctx context.Context
    35→
    36→	qs *queueSet
    37→
    38→	flowDistinguisher string
    39→	fsName            string
    40→
    41→	// The relevant queue.  Is nil if this request did not go through
    42→	// a queue.
    43→	queue *queue
    44→
    45→	// estimated amount of work of the request
    46→	workEstimate completedWorkEstimate
    47→
    48→	// decision gets set to a `requestDecision` indicating what to do
    49→	// with this request.  It gets set exactly once, when the request
    50→	// is removed from its queue.  The value will be decisionReject,
    51→	// decisionCancel, or decisionExecute.
    52→	//
    53→	// decision.Set is called with the queueSet locked.
    54→	// decision.Get is called without the queueSet locked.
    55→	decision promise.WriteOnce
    56→
    57→	// arrivalTime is the real time when the request entered this system
    58→	arrivalTime time.Time
    59→
    60→	// descr1 and descr2 are not used in any logic but they appear in
    61→	// log messages
    62→	descr1, descr2 interface{}
    63→
    64→	queueNoteFn fq.QueueNoteFn
    65→
    66→	// The preceding fields are filled in at creation and not modified since;
    67→	// the following fields may be modified later and must only be accessed while
    68→	// holding the queueSet's lock.
    69→
    70→	// Removes this request from its queue. If the request is not put into a
    71→	// a queue it will be nil.
    72→	removeFromQueueLocked removeFromFIFOFunc
    73→
    74→	// arrivalR is R(arrivalTime).  R is, confusingly, also called "virtual time".
    75→	// This field is meaningful only while the request is waiting in the virtual world.
    76→	arrivalR fcrequest.SeatSeconds
    77→
    78→	// startTime is the real time when the request began executing
    79→	startTime time.Time
    80→
    81→	// Indicates whether client has called Request::Wait()
    82→	waitStarted bool
    83→}
    84→
    85→type completedWorkEstimate struct {
    86→	fcrequest.WorkEstimate
    87→	totalWork fcrequest.SeatSeconds // initial plus final work
    88→	finalWork fcrequest.SeatSeconds // only final work
    89→}
    90→
    91→// queue is a sequence of requests that have arrived but not yet finished
    92→// execution in both the real and virtual worlds.
    93→type queue struct {
    94→	// The requestsWaiting not yet executing in the real world are stored in a FIFO list.
    95→	requestsWaiting fifo
    96→
    97→	// nextDispatchR is the R progress meter reading at
    98→	// which the next request will be dispatched in the virtual world.
    99→	nextDispatchR fcrequest.SeatSeconds
   100→
   101→	// requestsExecuting is the set of requests executing in the real world.
   102→	requestsExecuting sets.Set[*request]
   103→
   104→	// index is the position of this queue among those in its queueSet.
   105→	index int
   106→
   107→	// seatsInUse is the total number of "seats" currently occupied
   108→	// by all the requests that are currently executing in this queue.
   109→	seatsInUse int
   110→}
   111→
   112→// queueSum tracks the sum of initial seats, max seats, and
   113→// totalWork from all requests in a given queue
   114→type queueSum struct {
   115→	// InitialSeatsSum is the sum of InitialSeats
   116→	// associated with all requests in a given queue.
   117→	InitialSeatsSum int
   118→
   119→	// MaxSeatsSum is the sum of MaxSeats
   120→	// associated with all requests in a given queue.
   121→	MaxSeatsSum int
   122→
   123→	// TotalWorkSum is the sum of totalWork of the waiting requests
   124→	TotalWorkSum fcrequest.SeatSeconds
   125→}
   126→
   127→func (req *request) totalWork() fcrequest.SeatSeconds {
   128→	return req.workEstimate.totalWork
   129→}
   130→
   131→func (qs *queueSet) completeWorkEstimate(we *fcrequest.WorkEstimate) completedWorkEstimate {
   132→	finalWork := qs.computeFinalWork(we)
   133→	return completedWorkEstimate{
   134→		WorkEstimate: *we,
   135→		totalWork:    qs.computeInitialWork(we) + finalWork,
   136→		finalWork:    finalWork,
   137→	}
   138→}
   139→
   140→func (qs *queueSet) computeInitialWork(we *fcrequest.WorkEstimate) fcrequest.SeatSeconds {
   141→	return fcrequest.SeatsTimesDuration(float64(we.InitialSeats), qs.estimatedServiceDuration)
   142→}
   143→
   144→func (qs *queueSet) computeFinalWork(we *fcrequest.WorkEstimate) fcrequest.SeatSeconds {
   145→	return fcrequest.SeatsTimesDuration(float64(we.FinalSeats), we.AdditionalLatency)
   146→}
   147→
   148→func (q *queue) dumpLocked(includeDetails bool) debug.QueueDump {
   149→	waitingDigest := make([]debug.RequestDump, 0, q.requestsWaiting.Length())
   150→	q.requestsWaiting.Walk(func(r *request) bool {
   151→		waitingDigest = append(waitingDigest, dumpRequest(includeDetails)(r))
   152→		return true
   153→	})
   154→	executingDigest := SetMapReduce(dumpRequest(includeDetails), append1[debug.RequestDump])(q.requestsExecuting)
   155→
   156→	sum := q.requestsWaiting.QueueSum()
   157→	queueSum := debug.QueueSum{
   158→		InitialSeatsSum: sum.InitialSeatsSum,
   159→		MaxSeatsSum:     sum.MaxSeatsSum,
   160→		TotalWorkSum:    sum.TotalWorkSum.String(),
   161→	}
   162→
   163→	return debug.QueueDump{
   164→		NextDispatchR:     q.nextDispatchR.String(),
   165→		Requests:          waitingDigest,
   166→		RequestsExecuting: executingDigest,
   167→		ExecutingRequests: q.requestsExecuting.Len(),
   168→		SeatsInUse:        q.seatsInUse,
   169→		QueueSum:          queueSum,
   170→	}
   171→}
   172→
   173→func dumpRequest(includeDetails bool) func(*request) debug.RequestDump {
   174→	return func(r *request) debug.RequestDump {
   175→		ans := debug.RequestDump{
   176→			MatchedFlowSchema: r.fsName,
   177→			FlowDistinguisher: r.flowDistinguisher,
   178→			ArriveTime:        r.arrivalTime,
   179→			StartTime:         r.startTime,
   180→			WorkEstimate:      r.workEstimate.WorkEstimate,
   181→		}
   182→		if includeDetails {
   183→			userInfo, _ := genericrequest.UserFrom(r.ctx)
   184→			ans.UserName = userInfo.GetName()
   185→			requestInfo, ok := genericrequest.RequestInfoFrom(r.ctx)
   186→			if ok {
   187→				ans.RequestInfo = *requestInfo
   188→			}
   189→		}
   190→		return ans
   191→	}
   192→}
   193→
   194→// SetMapReduce is map-reduce starting from a set type in the sets package.
   195→func SetMapReduce[Elt comparable, Result, Accumulator any](mapFn func(Elt) Result, reduceFn func(Accumulator, Result) Accumulator) func(map[Elt]sets.Empty) Accumulator {
   196→	return func(set map[Elt]sets.Empty) Accumulator {
   197→		var ans Accumulator
   198→		for elt := range set {
   199→			ans = reduceFn(ans, mapFn(elt))
   200→		}
   201→		return ans
   202→	}
   203→}
   204→
   205→// SliceMapReduce is map-reduce starting from a slice.
   206→func SliceMapReduce[Elt, Result, Accumulator any](mapFn func(Elt) Result, reduceFn func(Accumulator, Result) Accumulator) func([]Elt) Accumulator {
   207→	return func(slice []Elt) Accumulator {
   208→		var ans Accumulator
   209→		for _, elt := range slice {
   210→			ans = reduceFn(ans, mapFn(elt))
   211→		}
   212→		return ans
   213→	}
   214→}
   215→
   216→func or(x, y bool) bool { return x || y }
   217→
   218→func append1[Elt any](slice []Elt, next Elt) []Elt { return append(slice, next) }
   219→

its queueSet.\n\tindex int\n\n\t// seatsInUse is the total number of \"seats\" currently occupied\n\t// by all the requests that are currently executing in this queue.\n\tseatsInUse int\n}\n\n// queueSum tracks the sum of initial seats, max seats, and\n// totalWork from all requests in a given queue\ntype queueSum struct {\n\t// InitialSeatsSum is the sum of InitialSeats\n\t// associated with all requests in a given queue.\n\tInitialSeatsSum int\n\n\t// MaxSeatsSum is the sum of MaxSeats\n\t// associated with all requests in a given queue.\n\tMaxSeatsSum int\n\n\t// TotalWorkSum is the sum of totalWork of the waiting requests\n\tTotalWorkSum fcrequest.SeatSeconds\n}\n\nfunc (req *request) totalWork() fcrequest.SeatSeconds {\n\treturn req.workEstimate.totalWork\n}\n\nfunc (qs *queueSet) completeWorkEstimate(we *fcrequest.WorkEstimate) completedWorkEstimate {\n\tfinalWork := qs.computeFinalWork(we)\n\treturn completedWorkEstimate{\n\t\tWorkEstimate: *we,\n\t\ttotalWork:    qs.computeInitialWork(we) + finalWork,\n\t\tfinalWork:    finalWork,\n\t}\n}\n\nfunc (qs *queueSet) computeInitialWork(we *fcrequest.WorkEstimate) fcrequest.SeatSeconds {\n\treturn fcrequest.SeatsTimesDuration(float64(we.InitialSeats), qs.estimatedServiceDuration)\n}\n\nfunc (qs *queueSet) computeFinalWork(we *fcrequest.WorkEstimate) fcrequest.SeatSeconds {\n\treturn fcrequest.SeatsTimesDuration(float64(we.FinalSeats), we.AdditionalLatency)\n}\n\nfunc (q *queue) dumpLocked(includeDetails bool) debug.QueueDump {\n\twaitingDigest := make([]debug.RequestDump, 0, q.requestsWaiting.Length())\n\tq.requestsWaiting.Walk(func(r *request) bool {\n\t\twaitingDigest = append(waitingDigest, dumpRequest(includeDetails)(r))\n\t\treturn true\n\t})\n\texecutingDigest := SetMapReduce(dumpRequest(includeDetails), append1[debug.RequestDump])(q.requestsExecuting)\n\n\tsum := q.requestsWaiting.QueueSum()\n\tqueueSum := debug.QueueSum{\n\t\tInitialSeatsSum: sum.InitialSeatsSum,\n\t\tMaxSeatsSum:     sum.MaxSeatsSum,\n\t\tTotalWorkSum:    sum.TotalWorkSum.String(),\n\t}\n\n\treturn debug.QueueDump{\n\t\tNextDispatchR:     q.nextDispatchR.String(),\n\t\tRequests:          waitingDigest,\n\t\tRequestsExecuting: executingDigest,\n\t\tExecutingRequests: q.requestsExecuting.Len(),\n\t\tSeatsInUse:        q.seatsInUse,\n\t\tQueueSum:          queueSum,\n\t}\n}\n\nfunc dumpRequest(includeDetails bool) func(*request) debug.RequestDump {\n\treturn func(r *request) debug.RequestDump {\n\t\tans := debug.RequestDump{\n\t\t\tMatchedFlowSchema: r.fsName,\n\t\t\tFlowDistinguisher: r.flowDistinguisher,\n\t\t\tArriveTime:        r.arrivalTime,\n\t\t\tStartTime:         r.startTime,\n\t\t\tWorkEstimate:      r.workEstimate.WorkEstimate,\n\t\t}\n\t\tif includeDetails {\n\t\t\tuserInfo, _ := genericrequest.UserFrom(r.ctx)\n\t\t\tans.UserName = userInfo.GetName()\n\t\t\trequestInfo, ok := genericrequest.RequestInfoFrom(r.ctx)\n\t\t\tif ok {\n\t\t\t\tans.RequestInfo = *requestInfo\n\t\t\t}\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn ans\n\t}\n}\n\n// SetMapReduce is map-reduce starting from a set type in the sets package.\nfunc SetMapReduce[Elt comparable, Result, Accumulator any](mapFn func(Elt) Result, reduceFn func(Accumulator, Result) Accumulator) func(map[Elt]sets.Empty) Accumulator {\n\treturn func(set map[Elt]sets.Empty) Accumulator {\n\t\tvar ans Accumulator\n\t\tfor elt := range set {\n\t\t\tans = reduceFn(ans, mapFn(elt))\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn ans\n\t}\n}\n\n// SliceMapReduce is map-reduce starting from a slice.\nfunc SliceMapReduce[Elt, Result, Accumulator any](mapFn func(Elt) Result, reduceFn func(Accumulator, Result) Accumulator) func([]Elt) Accumulator {\n\treturn func(slice []Elt) Accumulator {\n\t\tvar ans Accumulator\n\t\tfor _, elt := range slice {\n\t\t\tans = reduceFn(ans, mapFn(elt))\n\t\t}\n\t\treturn ans\n\t}\n}\n\nfunc or(x, y bool) bool { return x || y }\n\nfunc append1[Elt any](slice []Elt, next Elt) []Elt { return append(slice, next) }\n", "numLines": 219, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 219}}

    17→package fairqueuing
    18→
    19→import (
    20→	"context"
    21→
    22→	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/debug"
    23→	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/metrics"
    24→	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/util/flowcontrol/request"
    25→)
    26→
    27→// QueueSetFactory is used to create QueueSet objects.  Creation, like
    28→// config update, is done in two phases: the first phase consumes the
    29→// QueuingConfig and the second consumes the DispatchingConfig.  They
    30→// are separated so that errors from the first phase can be found
    31→// before committing to a concurrency allotment for the second.
    32→type QueueSetFactory interface {
    33→	// BeginConstruction does the first phase of creating a QueueSet.
    34→	// The RatioedGaugePair observes number of requests,
    35→	// execution covering just the regular phase.
    36→	// The denominator for the waiting phase is
    37→	// max(1, QueuingConfig.QueueLengthLimit) X max(1, QueuingConfig.DesiredNumQueues).
    38→	// The RatioedGauge observes number of seats occupied through all phases of execution.
    39→	// The denominator for all the ratioed concurrency gauges is supplied later in the DispatchingConfig.
    40→	// The Gauge observes the seat demand (executing + queued seats).
    41→	BeginConstruction(QueuingConfig, metrics.RatioedGaugePair, metrics.RatioedGauge, metrics.Gauge) (QueueSetCompleter, error)
    42→}
    43→
    44→// QueueSetCompleter finishes the two-step process of creating or
    45→// reconfiguring a QueueSet
    46→type QueueSetCompleter interface {
    47→	// Complete returns a QueueSet configured by the given
    48→	// dispatching configuration.
    49→	Complete(DispatchingConfig) QueueSet
    50→}
    51→
    52→// QueueSet is the abstraction for the queuing and dispatching
    53→// functionality of one non-exempt priority level.  It covers the
    54→// functionality described in the "Assignment to a Queue", "Queuing",
    55→// and "Dispatching" sections of
    56→// https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/1040-priority-and-fairness/README.md
    57→// .  Some day we may have connections between priority levels, but
    58→// today is not that day.
    59→type QueueSet interface {
    60→	// BeginConfigChange starts the two-step process of updating the
    61→	// configuration.  No change is made until Complete is called.  If
    62→	// `C := X.BeginConstruction(q)` then `C.Complete(d)` returns the
    63→	// same value `X`.  If the QueuingConfig's DesiredNumQueues field
    64→	// is zero then the other queuing-specific config parameters are
    65→	// not changed, so that the queues continue draining as before.
    66→	// In any case, reconfiguration does not discard any queue unless
    67→	// and until it is undesired and empty.
    68→	BeginConfigChange(QueuingConfig) (QueueSetCompleter, error)
    69→
    70→	// IsIdle returns a bool indicating whether the QueueSet was idle
    71→	// at the moment of the return.  Idle means the QueueSet has zero
    72→	// requests queued and zero executing.  This bit can change only
    73→	// (1) during a call to StartRequest and (2) during a call to
    74→	// Request::Finish.  In the latter case idleness can only change
    75→	// from false to true.
    76→	IsIdle() bool
    77→
    78→	// StartRequest begins the process of handling a request.  If the
    79→	// request gets queued and the number of queues is greater than 1
    80→	// then StartRequest uses the given hashValue as the source of
    81→	// entropy as it shuffle-shards the request into a queue.  The
    82→	// descr1 and descr2 values play no role in the logic but appear
    83→	// in log messages.  This method always returns quickly (without
    84→	// waiting for the request to be dequeued).  If this method
    85→	// returns a nil Request value then caller should reject the
    86→	// request and the returned bool indicates whether the QueueSet
    87→	// was idle at the moment of the return.  Otherwise idle==false
    88→	// and the client must call the Finish method of the Request
    89→	// exactly once.
    90→	StartRequest(ctx context.Context, width *request.WorkEstimate, hashValue uint64, flowDistinguisher, fsName string, descr1, descr2 interface{}, queueNoteFn QueueNoteFn) (req Request, idle bool)
    91→
    92→	// Dump saves and returns the instant internal state of the queue-set.
    93→	// Note that dumping process will stop the queue-set from proceeding
    94→	// any requests.
    95→	// For debugging only.
    96→	Dump(includeRequestDetails bool) debug.QueueSetDump
    97→}
    98→
    99→// QueueNoteFn is called when a request enters and leaves a queue
   100→type QueueNoteFn func(inQueue bool)
   101→
   102→// Request represents the remainder of the handling of one request
   103→type Request interface {
   104→	// Finish determines whether to execute or reject the request and
   105→	// invokes `execute` if the decision is to execute the request.
   106→	// The returned `idle bool` value indicates whether the QueueSet
   107→	// was idle when the value was calculated, but might no longer be
   108→	// accurate by the time the client examines that value.
   109→	Finish(execute func()) (idle bool)
   110→}
   111→
   112→// QueuingConfig defines the configuration of the queuing aspect of a QueueSet.
   113→type QueuingConfig struct {
   114→	// Name is used to identify a queue set, allowing for descriptive information about its intended use
   115→	Name string
   116→
   117→	// DesiredNumQueues is the number of queues that the API says
   118→	// should exist now.  This may be non-positive, in which case
   119→	// QueueLengthLimit, and HandSize are ignored.
   120→	// A value of zero means to respect the ConcurrencyLimit of the DispatchingConfig.
   121→	// A negative value means to always dispatch immediately upon arrival
   122→	// (i.e., the requests are "exempt" from limitation).
   123→	DesiredNumQueues int
   124→
   125→	// QueueLengthLimit is the maximum number of requests that may be waiting in a given queue at a time
   126→	QueueLengthLimit int
   127→
   128→	// HandSize is a parameter of shuffle sharding.  Upon arrival of a request, a queue is chosen by randomly
   129→	// dealing a "hand" of this many queues and then picking one of minimum length.
   130→	HandSize int
   131→}
   132→
   133→// DispatchingConfig defines the configuration of the dispatching aspect of a QueueSet.
   134→type DispatchingConfig struct {
   135→	// ConcurrencyLimit is the maximum number of requests of this QueueSet that may be executing at a time
   136→	ConcurrencyLimit int
   137→
   138→	// ConcurrencyDenominator is used in relative metrics of concurrency.
   139→	// It equals ConcurrencyLimit except when that is zero.
   140→	ConcurrencyDenominator int
   141→}
   142→

They\n// are separated so that errors from the first phase can be found\n// before committing to a concurrency allotment for the second.\ntype QueueSetFactory interface {\n\t// BeginConstruction does the first phase of creating a QueueSet.\n\t// The RatioedGaugePair observes number of requests,\n\t// execution covering just the regular phase.\n\t// The denominator for the waiting phase is\n\t// max(1, QueuingConfig.QueueLengthLimit) X max(1, QueuingConfig.DesiredNumQueues).\n\t// The RatioedGauge observes number of seats occupied through all phases of execution.\n\t// The denominator for all the ratioed concurrency gauges is supplied later in the DispatchingConfig.\n\t// The Gauge observes the seat demand (executing + queued seats).\n\tBeginConstruction(QueuingConfig, metrics.RatioedGaugePair, metrics.RatioedGauge, metrics.Gauge) (QueueSetCompleter, error)\n}\n\n// QueueSetCompleter finishes the two-step process of creating or\n// reconfiguring a QueueSet\ntype QueueSetCompleter interface {\n\t// Complete returns a QueueSet configured by the given\n\t// dispatching configuration.\n\tComplete(DispatchingConfig) QueueSet\n}\n\n// QueueSet is the abstraction for the queuing and dispatching\n// functionality of one non-exempt priority level.  It covers the\n// functionality described in the \"Assignment to a Queue\", \"Queuing\",\n// and \"Dispatching\" sections of\n// https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/1040-priority-and-fairness/README.md\n// .  Some day we may have connections between priority levels, but\n// today is not that day.\ntype QueueSet interface {\n\t// BeginConfigChange starts the two-step process of updating the\n\t// configuration.  No change is made until Complete is called.  If\n\t// `C := X.BeginConstruction(q)` then `C.Complete(d)` returns the\n\t// same value `X`.  If the QueuingConfig's DesiredNumQueues field\n\t// is zero then the other queuing-specific config parameters are\n\t// not changed, so that the queues continue draining as before.\n\t// In any case, reconfiguration does not discard any queue unless\n\t// and until it is undesired and empty.\n\tBeginConfigChange(QueuingConfig) (QueueSetCompleter, error)\n\n\t// IsIdle returns a bool indicating whether the QueueSet was idle\n\t// at the moment of the return.  Idle means the QueueSet has zero\n\t// requests queued and zero executing.  This bit can change only\n\t// (1) during a call to StartRequest and (2) during a call to\n\t// Request::Finish.  In the latter case idleness can only change\n\t// from false to true.\n\tIsIdle() bool\n\n\t// StartRequest begins the process of handling a request.  If the\n\t// request gets queued and the number of queues is greater than 1\n\t// then StartRequest uses the given hashValue as the source of\n\t// entropy as it shuffle-shards the request into a queue.  The\n\t// descr1 and descr2 values play no role in the logic but appear\n\t// in log messages.  This method always returns quickly (without\n\t// waiting for the request to be dequeued).  If this method\n\t// returns a nil Request value then caller should reject the\n\t// request and the returned bool indicates whether the QueueSet\n\t// was idle at the moment of the return.  Otherwise idle==false\n\t// and the client must call the Finish method of the Request\n\t// exactly once.\n\tStartRequest(ctx context.Context, width *request.WorkEstimate, hashValue uint64, flowDistinguisher, fsName string, descr1, descr2 interface{}, queueNoteFn QueueNoteFn) (req Request, idle bool)\n\n\t// Dump saves and returns the instant internal state of the queue-set.\n\t// Note that dumping process will stop the queue-set from proceeding\n\t// any requests.\n\t// For debugging only.\n\tDump(includeRequestDetails bool) debug.QueueSetDump\n}\n\n// QueueNoteFn is called when a request enters and leaves a queue\ntype QueueNoteFn func(inQueue bool)\n\n// Request represents the remainder of the handling of one request\ntype Request interface {\n\t// Finish determines whether to execute or reject the request and\n\t// invokes `execute` if the decision is to execute the request.\n\t// The returned `idle bool` value indicates whether the QueueSet\n\t// was idle when the value was calculated, but might no longer be\n\t// accurate by the time the client examines that value.\n\tFinish(execute func()) (idle bool)\n}\n\n// QueuingConfig defines the configuration of the queuing aspect of a QueueSet.\ntype QueuingConfig struct {\n\t// Name is used to identify a queue set, allowing for descriptive information about its intended use\n\tName string\n\n\t// DesiredNumQueues is the number of queues that the API says\n\t// should exist now.  This may be non-positive, in which case\n\t// QueueLengthLimit, and HandSize are ignored.\n\t// A value of zero means to respect the ConcurrencyLimit of the DispatchingConfig.\n\t// A negative value means to always dispatch immediately upon arrival\n\t// (i.e., the requests are \"exempt\" from limitation).\n\tDesiredNumQueues int\n\n\t// QueueLengthLimit is the maximum number of requests that may be waiting in a given queue at a time\n\tQueueLengthLimit int\n\n\t// HandSize is a parameter of shuffle sharding.
